items.q: Why is Jupiter classified as a gas giant?
items.correct: Its bulk is mostly hydrogen and helium with no defined solid surface.
items.misconception: It is mostly air like Earth's atmosphere.
items.why: Students project Earth's atmosphere onto Jupiter. Jupiter has no rocky surface - it's gas transitioning to metallic hydrogen with depth. Very different from a thin atmosphere around a solid planet.

items.correct: Earth's axial tilt - 23.5 degrees - causes different hemispheres to receive more direct sunlight at different times of year.
items.misconception: Earth is closer to the Sun in summer.
items.random_wrong: The Sun's intensity changes throughout the year.
items.why: Students assume distance causes seasons, but Earth is closest to the Sun in January (Northern Hemisphere winter). The TILT changes the angle of sunlight and day length - that's what drives seasons.

items.q: Why don't we have a solar eclipse every new moon?
items.correct: The Moon's orbit is tilted, so it usually passes above or below the Sun's apparent position.
items.why: Students think every new moon should eclipse the Sun. The Moon's orbit is tilted about 5 degrees to the ecliptic, so usually the new Moon doesn't align - alignment requires nodes lining up, giving eclipse seasons.

items.q: How many planets are in the Solar System (IAU 2006)?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 8. “12” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who proposed the heliocentric model in 1543?
items.why: Correct answer: Copernicus. “Galileo” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximately how many million kilometres is 1 AU?
items.why: Exact answer: 150 million km. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
